demographics: I am 19 years old. I am male. I am Black or African American. I have never been married. I completed high school (12th grade). My family income is $7,000 to $7,999. I am an independent (neither Democrat nor Republican). I am moderate (middle of the road). I have no religious affiliation. I attend religious services less than once a year.

events.event_text: At 17, I was let go from my after-school job at the local corner store. I'd been working there for over a year, helping with inventory and serving customers. The owner didn't give a reason, just said he didn't need me anymore.

When I was 17, my best friend of five years, who was Hispanic, moved away. We had grown up together, playing sports and hanging out after school. Suddenly, I found myself navigating high school without my closest companion. It was tough adjusting at first, but it opened my eyes to how interconnected our diverse community really is.

At 18, I witnessed the local police stop and frisk my cousin, who's Black like me, for no apparent reason. He did nothing wrong, but they treated him like a criminal. It made me realize that I, too, could be targeted just because of my skin color.

At 18, I started volunteering at a local community center to stay busy after my friend moved away. I was paired with an older mentor, Ms. Thompson, who was White and taught me about civic engagement and social justice issues. We worked together on a weekly basis, organizing food drives and teaching workshops for local kids. It was eye-opening to see the extent of racial disparities in our community, and Ms. Thompson became like a second mother to me.

At 18, I was walking home from school when a police car pulled up next to me. The officer asked me to step out of the car, searched me, and questioned me for over an hour, all because I fit the description of a crime I didn't even know about. It was scary and humiliating, and it really opened my eyes to the reality of racial profiling.

At 16, I started working at a fast food joint to help out with bills. Despite being the most reliable employee, I was only making $7.25 an hour. It was exhausting and frustrating, but it really opened my eyes to how wage theft and unfair labor practices can impact people, especially those from lower-income backgrounds like mine.

At 18, I was walking home from volunteering at the community center when I witnessed a group of kids, younger than me, getting harassed by a group of teens from a rival gang. They were scared, and I remembered how lonely and desperate I felt when my best friend left. I stepped in, talked to them calmly, and walked them home safely, even though it put me in a tough spot with the other gang. It was terrifying, but it made me realize I wanted to work with youth at risk.

compressed_summaries.summary: This person, at 17, lost their job at a local corner store without explanation, and shortly after, their best friend moved away, forcing them to navigate high school alone. At 18, they witnessed racial profiling against their cousin, leading them to volunteer at a community center where they were mentored by a white woman, exposing them to social justice issues and racial disparities.

This person, at 18, began volunteering at a community center, learning about social justice from a White mentor while witnessing racial disparities. They experienced racial profiling firsthand when stopped by police, and at 16, working at a fast food joint, they saw wage theft's impact on lower-income employees. At 18, they intervened to protect younger kids from gang harassment, deciding to work with at-risk youth.

persona_card: This 19-year-old Black male, raised in a low-income household, is unaffiliated with any religion and rarely attends services. He holds progressive economic views, advocating for redistribution and a strong government role in addressing class disparities. With a structuralist perspective on race, he recognizes systemic barriers faced by minorities, shaped by personal experiences with racial profiling and wage theft. Despite his mistrust in institutions due to personal experiences, he remains open to diverse social outgroups, fostering a welcoming attitude towards immigrants, racial minorities, and LGBTQ individuals, while maintaining traditional moral views.
